use_cases.description: HR and compliance teams verify the authenticity of submitted written work such as applications or reports.

features: Per-seat pricing for schools and organizations

enrichment.short_description: gql.tada is a free, open-source TypeScript library that infers fully-typed GraphQL query results directly from your schema, with no code generation step.
enrichment.seo_title: gql.tada Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: gql.tada is a free, open-source TypeScript library for zero-codegen, type-safe GraphQL queries. See features, setup, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
gql.tada, styled with a magic-wand emoji as 'gql.tada', is an open-source TypeScript library described by its own documentation as a 'GraphQL document authoring library.' It infers the result and variable types of GraphQL queries and fragments directly within the TypeScript type system, letting developers write GraphQL documents as tagged template strings in their source code and receive fully accurate, live-updating types without ever running a separate code-generation build step.

The project is developed and maintained by 0no.co, an open-source-focused organization operating in the GraphQL and TypeScript tooling space. It is closely paired with a companion project, GraphQLSP, a TypeScript language server plugin that surfaces schema-aware diagnostics, autocompletion, and hover documentation directly inside code editors such as VS Code whenever a developer is writing a GraphQL document. Both gql.tada and GraphQLSP are published as open-source packages on npm and developed publicly on GitHub under the 0no-co organization, and both are free to use with no commercial licensing tier.

What sets gql.tada apart architecturally is where and when its type inference happens. Traditional GraphQL type-safety tooling, most notably GraphQL Code Generator, works by running a separate command-line step that reads your schema and your GraphQL operations and then writes out generated .ts or .d.ts files containing the corresponding TypeScript types; those generated files then have to be re-run and kept in sync every time the schema or a query changes, which can create friction or subtly stale types if a developer forgets to regenerate. gql.tada instead performs all of its inference live, inside the TypeScript compiler and type checker itself: it parses each GraphQL document string as a type-level abstract syntax tree, cross-references it against a cached, introspected copy of the target schema (along with any custom scalar type mappings you've configured), and derives the resulting query and variable types purely through TypeScript's own type system. The practical effect is that types are always accurate and immediately reflect the current state of a query as a developer types it, with no generated files to go stale and no build step to remember to re-run.

On top of this core inference engine, gql.tada includes several developer-experience features. GraphQLSP integration gives schema-aware autocompletion and inline diagnostics directly inside GraphQL template literals as you write them, essentially turning a plain string into a fully IDE-supported mini-language. A feature called 'fragment masking' enforces that any component or function consuming a GraphQL fragment must explicitly unwrap or 'read' that fragment's data rather than reaching directly into its fields, a pattern borrowed from the Relay and modern urql ecosystems that encourages cleaner, more maintainable component-level data-fetching boundaries. The library and its APIs are also fully self-documented via TSDoc comments, so hovering over any gql.tada function or type in a supporting editor surfaces its documentation inline without needing to leave the code. On the client-integration side, gql.tada is most tightly built to interoperate with urql, a popular lightweight GraphQL client, though its type-inference core is not fundamentally tied to any single client and can be adapted to others.

Pricing is straightforward: gql.tada is completely free and open source, distributed via npm with no paid tier, subscription, or commercial license fee of any kind, consistent with the norms of the broader open-source GraphQL and TypeScript tooling ecosystem it belongs to. There is no separate enterprise offering or hosted service — it is purely a development-time library that runs inside a project's own TypeScript toolchain.

gql.tada's target audience is frontend and full-stack TypeScript developers working against GraphQL APIs who want compile-time type safety for their queries without the operational overhead of a codegen pipeline. It resonates particularly strongly with teams already using or evaluating the urql GraphQL client, and with developers who have found the traditional GraphQL Code Generator workflow cumbersome to keep synchronized during fast-moving development, where schema and query changes happen frequently. Competitively, gql.tada's main point of comparison is GraphQL Code Generator itself, the long-established standard for typing GraphQL operations in TypeScript via a build-time CLI step; gql.tada's core pitch is that removing that build step entirely, in favor of native type-system inference, gives instant and always-accurate types as you edit — at the tradeoff of being a comparatively newer and smaller project with a narrower plugin and preset ecosystem than the mature Codegen toolchain. It also shares some conceptual DNA with Relay's compiler-driven approach to type safety and with GraphQL clients that ship their own typed-document systems, but distinguishes itself by working through ordinary TypeScript tooling rather than requiring a dedicated build plugin, babel transform, or swc plugin to function.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: gql.tada is a free, open-source TypeScript library that infers fully accurate result and variable types for GraphQL queries and fragments directly within the TypeScript type system, eliminating the need for a separate code-generation build step.

Maintained by 0no.co and paired with the companion GraphQLSP language server plugin, gql.tada gives developers live, schema-aware autocompletion and type checking for GraphQL documents written as tagged template strings, right inside their editor.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Because gql.tada performs type inference live inside the TypeScript compiler rather than through a generated-file pipeline, query and variable types are always in sync with the current schema and query text, with nothing to regenerate or go stale.

Features include schema-aware autocompletion via GraphQLSP, 'fragment masking' to enforce clean component-level data boundaries, full TSDoc self-documentation surfaced on hover, and tight interoperability with the urql GraphQL client.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: gql.tada is completely free and open source, distributed via npm with no paid tier, subscription, or commercial license.
There is no hosted service or enterprise edition; it is purely a development-time library that plugs into a project's existing TypeScript toolchain at no cost.

features.title: Zero-codegen type inference
features.description: Infers GraphQL query and variable types live inside the TypeScript type system, with no separate build step to generate files.

features.title: Schema-aware autocomplete via GraphQLSP
features.description: A companion language server plugin provides in-editor autocompletion and diagnostics for GraphQL documents.

features.title: Fragment masking
features.description: Enforces that consumers explicitly unwrap fragment data, encouraging cleaner, component-scoped data-fetching patterns.

features.title: TSDoc self-documentation
features.description: All APIs are documented with TSDocs, surfacing inline documentation on hover in supporting editors.

features.title: urql interoperability
features.description: Built to work closely with the urql GraphQL client, with adaptability to other GraphQL clients.

features.title: Live editor diagnostics
features.description: Flags invalid queries and type mismatches directly in the editor as you write GraphQL documents.

features.title: Tagged template literal syntax
features.description: GraphQL documents are authored as plain TypeScript tagged template strings, requiring no new file format.

features.title: npm-distributed open source package
features.description: Installed as a standard npm dependency with no proprietary tooling or account required.

pros: No build or codegen step to maintain or forget to re-run
Types are always accurate and immediately in sync with source changes
Tight editor integration via the GraphQLSP language server plugin
Completely free and open source
Encourages cleaner fragment-based data-fetching patterns
cons: Smaller, newer ecosystem than the long-established GraphQL Code Generator
Best editor experience currently depends on TypeScript-aware language server support
Tightest first-class integration is with urql rather than every GraphQL client
Debugging type inference issues requires understanding TypeScript's type system internals
Less community documentation and tutorial content than more mature codegen tooling

faqs.question: What is gql.tada?
faqs.answer: gql.tada is a free, open-source TypeScript library that infers fully-typed GraphQL query and variable types directly from your schema, without requiring a separate code-generation step.

faqs.question: Is gql.tada free?
faqs.answer: Yes, gql.tada is completely free and open source, distributed via npm with no paid tier or commercial license.

faqs.question: Do I still need a build step to use gql.tada?
faqs.answer: No separate code-generation build step is required; type inference happens live inside the TypeScript compiler as you write GraphQL documents.

faqs.question: Does gql.tada work with Apollo Client?
faqs.answer: gql.tada's core type-inference engine is client-agnostic, though it is most tightly built and documented for use with the urql GraphQL client.

faqs.question: What is GraphQLSP?
faqs.answer: GraphQLSP is a companion TypeScript language server plugin, also maintained by 0no.co, that provides schema-aware autocompletion and diagnostics for GraphQL documents inside supporting editors.

faqs.question: What is fragment masking in gql.tada?
faqs.answer: Fragment masking is a pattern that requires consumers of a GraphQL fragment to explicitly unwrap its data rather than accessing fields directly, encouraging cleaner component-level data boundaries.

use_cases.title: Type-safe GraphQL queries with urql
use_cases.description: TypeScript frontend applications using the urql client adopt gql.tada for live, accurate query typing.

use_cases.title: Removing a GraphQL codegen build step
use_cases.description: Teams replace a traditional GraphQL Code Generator pipeline with gql.tada's live type inference to simplify their build process.

use_cases.title: Fragment-based component data requirements
use_cases.description: Library and component authors use fragment masking to define clean, type-safe data dependencies for reusable components.
use_cases.audience: Component library authors and design system teams

enrichment.short_description: Grade.us is a white-label review management and reputation marketing platform built for marketing agencies to generate, monitor, and market client reviews.
enrichment.seo_title: Grade.us Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Grade.us is a white-label review management platform for agencies. See 2026 pricing plans, features, pros and cons, and top Grade.us alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Grade.us is a white-label online review management and reputation marketing platform purpose-built for marketing agencies, consultants, and SEOs to handle review generation, monitoring, and marketing on behalf of the client businesses they serve. Unlike consumer-facing or single-location review tools aimed at an individual brand managing its own reputation, Grade.us is architected from the ground up around the agency business model, where one operator manages review programs across many separate client accounts under their own brand.

The platform was founded in 2013 and operated independently for several years before becoming a subsidiary of Traject in 2020, a company that also owns a portfolio of other local-marketing and social-media SaaS tools. Grade.us is headquartered in Montclair, New Jersey, and continues to operate under its own brand as part of the Traject family of products.

Grade.us's central value proposition is white-labeling: agencies can fully rebrand the platform with their own logo, color scheme, and a unique custom domain, so that end clients interacting with review request emails, dashboards, and widgets see the agency's own brand rather than 'Grade.us' anywhere in the experience. This lets agencies resell review management as a distinct, billable service line to their existing client base, and the company markets the platform on the basis of the healthy profit margins agencies can realize by reselling it rather than building review-management tooling in-house.

On the feature side, Grade.us centers on a few core workflows. Automated review request campaigns are sent to a business's customers via both email and SMS text message, guiding satisfied customers toward leaving public reviews on the third-party sites that matter most for that business (Google, Facebook, and various industry-specific directories), while routing customers who indicate dissatisfaction into a private feedback and recovery flow instead of a public review page — a common 'review gating' pattern designed to protect public reputation while still capturing negative feedback for internal follow-up. Beyond generation, the platform provides centralized monitoring of reviews across dozens of third-party review sites in one dashboard, letting an agency track new reviews (including negative ones) and respond promptly across all of its clients from a single place. For marketing reviews once collected, Grade.us offers embeddable review widgets and a review carousel that clients can place on their own websites to display social proof and support local SEO, along with reporting and analytics tools agencies use to demonstrate the ROI of the service back to their clients.

Pricing follows a per-seat, tiered subscription model rather than a flat single price. The Partner plan starts at roughly $25 per seat per month, Agency at roughly $40 per seat per month, Professional at roughly $60 per seat per month, and Solo at roughly $110 per seat per month, with the exact tier a customer needs depending on the number of users and clients they intend to manage. SMS text message review request campaigns are billed as an add-on on top of the base plan, at roughly $10 per month per 200 messages. Grade.us offers a 14-day free trial with no credit card required to start, and the company advertises that there are no long-term contracts, allowing customers to cancel at any time.

The platform's target audience is squarely digital marketing agencies and SEO consultancies that manage reputation and review generation for multiple small-business clients simultaneously, particularly in local-service verticals such as home services, healthcare practices, and retail. Company-published data cited by Grade.us notes that a large share of its user base comes from small companies, with a notable concentration of reviewers working in marketing and advertising roles, reinforcing its identity as an agency-first tool rather than a direct-to-business product.

Within the reputation and review management category, Grade.us competes with platforms like Birdeye, Podium, ReviewTrackers, and NiceJob, as well as broader local-marketing suites that bundle review management alongside other services. Its clearest differentiator is the agency-first, white-label business model: rather than selling directly to end businesses the way most competitors do, Grade.us is deliberately structured so that agencies can resell it under their own brand as a recurring revenue line, which in turn shapes its per-seat, multi-client pricing structure, its emphasis on client-facing reporting, and its overall positioning as infrastructure for building a reputation-management service line rather than a stand-alone consumer-facing product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Grade.us is a white-label review management and reputation marketing platform built specifically for marketing agencies, consultants, and SEOs to manage review generation, monitoring, and marketing on behalf of their client businesses.

Founded in 2013 and headquartered in Montclair, New Jersey, Grade.us became a subsidiary of Traject in 2020 and continues to operate as an agency-first product distinct from single-brand review tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core workflows include automated email and SMS review request campaigns that route unhappy customers into a private feedback flow, centralized multi-site review monitoring across dozens of third-party review platforms, and embeddable review widgets for client websites.
The platform's defining feature is full white-labeling, including custom branding and a unique domain, letting agencies resell Grade.us under their own brand to their client base.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Grade.us prices per seat per month across four tiers: Partner (~$25), Agency (~$40), Professional (~$60), and Solo (~$110), with SMS review request campaigns billed as a separate add-on.
A 14-day free trial with no credit card required is available, and the company advertises no long-term contracts, allowing cancellation at any time.

features.title: Automated email and SMS review requests
features.description: Sends review request campaigns to customers via email and text message to funnel them toward leaving public reviews.

features.title: Negative review recovery funnel
features.description: Routes dissatisfied customers into a private feedback flow instead of a public review page, protecting online reputation.

features.title: Multi-site review monitoring dashboard
features.description: Tracks and centralizes new reviews from dozens of third-party review sites in one place.

features.title: White-label branding and custom domain
features.description: Lets agencies rebrand the entire platform with their own logo and domain for client-facing use.

features.title: Review widget and carousel
features.description: Embeddable widgets display collected reviews on a client's website for SEO and social proof.

features.title: Client reporting and analytics
features.description: Provides reporting tools agencies can use to demonstrate review-management ROI to their clients.

features.title: Agency reseller model
features.description: Structured for agencies to resell review management as a billable service line under their own brand.

features.title: Major review site integrations
features.description: Connects with review platforms including Google and Facebook alongside industry-specific directories.

pros: Purpose-built white-label model lets agencies resell as their own product
Negative-review recovery funnel helps protect public reputation
Centralized multi-client dashboard saves agencies time managing many accounts
14-day free trial with no credit card required
No long-term contracts, with cancellation available at any time
cons: Per-seat pricing can add up for agencies managing many clients
SMS review request campaigns cost extra on top of the base plan
Pricing sits higher than some single-location review tools
Primarily agency-oriented, making it less ideal for a single independent business
Some users report price increases and a preference for annual billing

faqs.question: What is Grade.us?
faqs.answer: Grade.us is a white-label review management and reputation marketing platform built for marketing agencies to generate, monitor, and market reviews on behalf of their clients.

faqs.question: Who is Grade.us designed for?
faqs.answer: Grade.us is designed primarily for digital marketing agencies and SEO consultancies managing review programs for multiple small-business clients, rather than for a single business managing its own reviews.

faqs.question: How much does Grade.us cost?
faqs.answer: Grade.us prices per seat per month, starting at roughly $25/seat/month for the Partner plan and scaling up to Agency, Professional, and Solo tiers, with SMS campaigns billed as an add-on.

faqs.question: Can I white-label Grade.us under my own agency brand?
faqs.answer: Yes, Grade.us can be fully white-labeled with an agency's own logo, branding, and a custom domain so clients never see the Grade.us name.

faqs.question: Does Grade.us send SMS review requests?
faqs.answer: Yes, Grade.us supports SMS text message review request campaigns in addition to email, billed as a separate add-on on top of the base plan.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial for Grade.us?
faqs.answer: Yes, Grade.us offers a 14-day free trial with no credit card required to get started.

use_cases.title: Agency review management reselling
use_cases.description: Marketing agencies resell review management as a white-labeled, billable service to their local-business clients.

use_cases.title: Local SEO review building
use_cases.description: SEO consultants use review generation and widgets to strengthen a client's local search visibility.

use_cases.title: Multi-location reputation monitoring
use_cases.description: Businesses with multiple locations centralize review monitoring and response across all sites in one dashboard.
